title: Potted shrimp
subtitle: Brown shrimp set in spiced butter
excerpt: Brown shrimp from Morecambe Bay folded into clarified butter spiced with mace, cayenne, and nutmeg, then set in small pots. One of the oldest and most celebrated British seafood preparations.
sourceNotes: Potted shrimp is one of the most distinctive dishes in the British culinary tradition. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery (1747), documents potted shrimp as a standard British preparation. The Morecambe Bay brown shrimp version became the most celebrated form, documented throughout the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Mrs Beeton also includes potted shrimp in Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136.

content.text: Potted shrimp requires brown shrimp: the tiny, sweet, pre-cooked shrimp from Morecambe Bay or similar cold waters. They are available from good fishmongers and online. Larger prawns can be substituted but the result is a different dish with a less delicate flavour. The butter must be properly clarified so that the set is clean and the shrimp are suspended rather than sinking.

The spicing is traditional and precise: mace is the dominant note, supported by a little cayenne and nutmeg. The proportions are deliberately restrained, the butter should taste of shrimp first, spice second. Serve with hot toast that has been allowed to dry briefly so it does not go soggy under the cold butter.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter over very low heat until it separates: the foam rises to the top and the milky solids sink to the bottom. Carefully spoon off the foam. Pour the clear golden butter through a fine sieve or a piece of muslin into a clean pan, leaving the white solids behind.

Return the clarified butter to very low heat. Add the mace, cayenne, and nutmeg. Stir in {{brown-shrimp}} of cooked, peeled brown shrimp and add {{lemon-juice}} of lemon juice. Warm gently for 2 minutes, do not let the butter bubble or the shrimp will overcook. Taste and season.

Divide the shrimp mixture between four small pots or ramekins. Leave to cool for 10 minutes, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours until the butter has set firm. To serve, bring to room temperature for 15 minutes. Serve with hot toast.

items.fix: Let the mixture cool for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, before potting. The butter should have begun to thicken slightly before it goes in.
items.cause: Butter was still too liquid when poured into the pots
items.symptom: Shrimp have sunk to the bottom of the pot

items.fix: Clarify more thoroughly next time. The white solids do not affect flavour but produce a less clean, less professional set.
items.cause: Clarification was incomplete, milk solids were not fully separated
items.symptom: Butter has a cloudy white layer at the bottom

items.fix: The mace and cayenne should be present but not dominant. Taste the warm butter before potting and increase the spices as needed.
Toast the bread, then leave it on a rack for 1 to 2 minutes before serving so some moisture escapes. Very fresh toast and cold butter is not a good combination.
items.cause: Toast was too fresh and moist

content.text: Potted shrimp is one of the oldest continuously made British dishes. The cookery writer Hannah Glasse documented it in 1747. It has appeared in British cookery books ever since. The Morecambe Bay version became the most famous. The tiny, sweet brown shrimp from the shallow bay on the Lancashire coast have been potted commercially since the 1800s. The small family firms that pot them by hand have made the dish a regional speciality with a reputation well beyond the area. The spiced butter and sweet shrimp work so well together that the combination has survived nearly three centuries almost unchanged.
